doctrine: Every content-addressed id in the protocol (vf_ finding, vev_ canonical event, vpr_ proposal, the snapshot hash, the event-log leaf) is the SHA-256 of the canonical JSON bytes of a value. Two implementations derive the same id from the same logical content only if their canonicalization is byte-identical. These vectors pin vela.canonical-json/v1 so any implementation can verify byte-for-byte: keys sorted lexicographically at every depth, no inter-token whitespace, UTF-8 preserved verbatim (NOT \u-escaped), floats in serde_json/Ryu shortest-round-trip form (1.0 keeps its decimal point), array order preserved, non-finite floats rejected.

conforming_implementations: Python: vela_verify_log.canonical_bytes (clients/python/vela_verify_log.py) -- json.dumps(sort_keys=True, separators=(',',':'), ensure_ascii=False). This is the load-bearing path: it recomputes each vev_ id and refuses to trust the hub's leaves.
explicitly_out_of_scope: TypeScript: canonicalJson in clients/typescript/vela_reducer.ts is NOT a content-addressing function and does NOT conform. It is used only to compare reducer effect-rows for cross-impl parity, where both sides are canonicalized by the same function so any divergence cancels. It diverges on floats (JSON.stringify renders 1.0 as `1`, no int/float distinction) and MUST NOT be used to derive or check a content-addressed id. The effect-row shape deliberately types numeric fields (e.g. confidence_score) as strings so no float ever enters that comparison.

Python default json.dumps WITHOUT ensure_ascii=False renders non-ASCII as \uXXXX (e.g. amyloid-\u03b2) and therefore does NOT conform. Any Python content-addressing path MUST pass ensure_ascii=False, as vela_verify_log.canonical_bytes does.

vectors.name: object keys sort at every depth
floats render with a decimal point (content-address form)
empty containers and null pass through
array order preserved while sibling keys sort
realistic event content preimage (10 fields, unicode in key)
